Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. ___ was followed by psychiatry and social work 
services, and remained with a sitter throughout her hospital 
stay. On ___ she became acutely agitated. Cesarean section 
was performed on this day. During the surgery an incidental 
finding of a left paratubal cyst was noted; this was removed at 
the same time. 

Her postoperative course was unremarkable. She remained afebrile 
throughout. On postoperative day 4 she was tolerating a regular 
diet, able to ambulate and void normally, and tolerating po pain 
medications with good control of her pain. She is medically 
cleared to be discharged from the postpartum unit to an 
inpatient psychiatric facility.

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted for shortened cervix, which had been 
followed by ___ since her full fetal survey.  She was started on 
48h course of betamethazone, which she completed on day of 
discharge (HD#3).  Fetal well being was evaluated by BPP and 
daily NSTs, which were all reassuring.  Of note, the patient has

which she has also seen MFM, and declined quad or an amnio.

declined.  Pt was offered to stay for at least a week by Dr. 
___ pt declined.  Pt reports difficult child care

bedrest and to have another cervical length check in one week 
(scheduled ___.

history as well as her life stressors at present, including 
child care issues.

History of Present Illness:
___ yo G3P1 at 7w3d by Clomid/IUI dating presenting with vaginal 
bleeding and possible cervical ectopic pregnancy on outside 
imaging today. She has had intermittent vaginal bleeding, with 
outside ultrasound on ___ showing a live SIUP, size equal to 
dates, with a sub-chorionic hematoma. She had a follow-up 
ultrasound within the past week that also showed a live SIUP, 
but with resolution of the hematoma. However, today she called 
to report heavy vaginal bleeding with some clots and "tissue," 
and had an ultrasound that was concerning for a cervical ectopic 
pregnancy with 2 gestational sacs seen - 1 intra-uterine and 1 
in the cervix. She was referred to the ED for further 
evaluation. At present she reports mild cramping/soreness but is 
overall comfortable, with decreased bleeding from earlier in the 
day. Per the ED resident, on pelvic exam the cervix is open with 
clot/?POC seen within the os, and a small amount of blood in the 
vaginal vault.

.
Early OB U/S < 14 weeks; TV OB U/S (___):
IMPRESSION: 
1. Single living intrauterine gestation with size equals dates. 
2. Gestational sac within the cervix is unchanged in size and 
position.  
Non-living embryonic pole size measures 1 week less than dates 
with no cardiac activity. Again, this could represent a 
spontaneous abortion in progress versus a cervical ectopic. 
Close clinical correlation with beta hCG and/or ultrasound 
followup is recommended. 
3. Fibroid uterus.
.

 
Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. ___ was admitted for observation given her heterotopic 
cervical pregnancy and vaginal bleeding. Her bleeding slowed and 
on hospital day #3, she underwent an uncomplicated removal of 
the cervical ectopic pregnancy with placement of an 
intra-cervical Foley balloon and Prolene stitch as cerclage 
given the steady, slow bleeding post-procedure; see operative 
report for details. She had an uncomplicated recovery with 
removal of the Foley/cerclage and discharge home on hospital day 
#5, post-operative day #2 in stable condition, with live IUP 
seen on u/s following Foley balloon removal the day of 
discharge. 

 
Medications on Admission:
Pre-natal vitamins
 
Discharge Medications:
1. Prenatal  Oral
2. acetaminophen 500 mg Tablet Sig: ___ Tablets PO Q6H (every 6 
hours) as needed for pain.  

 
Discharge Disposition:
Home
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Cervical ectopic pregnancy and live intra-uterine pregnancy
Vaginal bleeding

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. ___,

It was a pleasure to take care of you while you were in the 
hospital. You were admitted because you told us about vaginal 
bleeding. On your third day of hospitalization, we took you to 
the operating room and removed the products of ectopic pregnancy 
from the cervix. Your intra-uterine pregnancy was confirmed to 
be live pre- and post-procedure using ultrasound imaging. To 
stop the steady, slow bleeding we saw after the procedure, we 
placed a balloon catheter in your cervix with two stitches to 
hold the balloon in place. After two days, we removed both the 
catheter and the stitches. You recovered well from the 
procedure, with pain well-controlled on no medications. 
.
General instructions:
* A small amount of vaginal spotting over the next few days is 
normal.
* Take your medications as prescribed.
* Do not drive while taking narcotics.
* Do not take more than 4000mg acetaminophen (APAP) in 24 hrs.
* No strenuous activity, nothing in the vagina (no tampons, no 
douching, no sex), no heavy lifting of objects >10lbs for 6 
weeks. 
* You may eat a regular diet.

To reach medical records to get the records from this 
hospitalization sent to your doctor at home, call ___.

monitoring.

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. ___ is a ___ Behcet's, Crohn's disease on

# Abdominal pain
# Behcet's 
# Crohn disease with TI
Patient presenting with progressively worsening epigastric 
abdominal pain. Given therapy with prednisone and Remicade, 
esophagitis was a consideration. Patient also with history of 
gastritis with metaplasia. However, EGD overall normal and does 
not seem to explain her current symptoms. Recent colonoscopy 
without evidence of active Crohn disease, so this is also less 
likely. Low suspicion for primary cardiac/pulmonary etiology 
given lack of risk factors, reproducibility of pain on exam, EKG 
without ischemic changes, trop negative, and CTA negative. Given 
extensive GI workup, likely significant etiology is function 
dyspepsia with visceral hypersensitivity and/or mixed IBS. The 
latter is supported with cycles of diarrhea and constipation 
with expected relief from defecation.  Some symptoms with 
features of gastroparesis and emptying could be considered in 
the outpatient setting. Patient followed by GI during her stay 
who emphasized management of symptoms as the cornerstone of her 
care rather than ongoing testing. There was an emphasis on 
ensure patient had normal bowel movements. She was reassured 
multiple times that no concerning pathology was identified, but 
also acknowledging that her symptoms were very distress. She 
will require ongoing close management with her outpatient GI 
doctor and PCP. She was given 3 tabs of oxycodone 5 mg to use 
only for severe abdominal pain. She was also prescribed a trial

had mild AST and ALT elevation. Her pain regimen has gradually 
increased from tylenol # 3 to oxycontin 40mg BID and PCA MSIV 
with 42mg of narcotic from 8am to 5pm. She has also been febrile 
to 102 starting on ___. Infectious w/u included negative chest 
xray, positive UA but cult < 10 K organisms, and negative blood 
culture. She was empirically given CTX for ? 4 doses in the 
periop period. She has had progressive tachycardia in the 
setting of hemolysis. The patient has refused transfusion. 
.
___ she was transfered from OB to HMED. Shortly after arrival 
she was somulent, tachycardic to the 130s, hypotensive to 90/60, 
tachypneic to 30, and desating to 88% on RA. She received 0.4mg 
of narcan with signficiant improvement in BP and MS, but she

.

ICU / Medical ward course:
A/P:  ___ y/o with sickle/B thal s/p uncomplicated vaginal 
delivery 4 days ago with progress anemia and AMS
.
1. Anemia, likely secondary to sickle cell crisis: consistent 
with hemolysis from sickle cell crisis as manifested by elevated 
total bili, decreased haptoglobin. The lack of significant past 
pain crises is unusual. No evidence of active hemorrhage. The 
patient was tranfused with 4 units total of RBC during her stay. 
She received IV fluids and oxygen. Blood smears and DIC labs 
were all sent. Given the development of a RLL infiltrate and 
altered mental status, consideration was given to complications 
such as acute chest syndrome and stroke. The head CT made stroke 
unlikely, and the patient's chest ___ was not consistent with 
acute chest syndrome.
  
2. Altered Mental Status: Probably multifactorial, but most 
heavily dependent on patient given too much narcotic, especially 
since the somulence partially reversed with Narcan. The patient 
did remain altered for a period of days. Her CT head was 
negative for acute acute pathology. Given fevers, infection may 
have been an additonal possibility, and there was initial 
concern, given the patient's recent history that she may have an 
epidural abscess or encephalitis secondary to the epidural she 
had giving birth. CT head wet read without acute pathology. The 
patient's delirium began to dissipate during her third day in 
the ICU.
.
3. Fevers: The patient had five days of consistent fever. Her 
CXR appeared with new RLL opacity, possibly a HAP vs aspiration 
in the setting of AMS. Postpartum retained products of 
conception or endometritis must be considered, although she has 
no vaginal bleeding or tenderness on palpation of the uterus. UA 
positive but culture negative, ? underdiagnosis of UTI. The 
blood, sputum, urine cultures were all taken and had no growth.  
Abdominal US to look for products of conception vs obvious 
endometritis showed neither. The patient was placed on a course 
of vancomycin and Zosyn to cover for HAP and endometritis. 
.
4. RLL opacity: Fluid vs PNA vs acute chest. Without severe 
hypoxia or chest pain, acute chest less likely. The patient 
received vanco and zosyn, given altered mental status and 
possible aspiration. If the patient had had worsening resp 
status, we would have consulted transfusion medicine for 
exchange transfusion for acute chest syndrome. This was 
unnecessary.
.
5. Elevated LFTs: mild ALT, AST, tbili, LDH elevation. Can be 
consistent with hemolysis. In the setting of mild 
thrombocytopenia (which can be seen with sickle crisis), raises 
concern for HELLP syndrome. However, the patient was without 
preeclampsia, and LFTs expected to be significantly higher in 
HELLP.  Also, definitive therapy is delivery of infant, which 
occured. 
.
6. Hypertension:  She was significantly hypertensive post partum 
on the medical floor requiring carvediolol, lisinopril and lasix 
regimen.  ECHO showed RV pressure/filling overload, EF 45-50% 
and mild RV hypokinesis.  She was without need for O2 and 
ambulating well without tachycardia.  She was referred to 
cardiology appointment outpatient.   BP well controlled on 
discharge with regimen as per discharge meds.  She will have f/u 
BP check soon
.
7. Pending labs at discharge:  HIV antibody test.
.

group therapy, and she was forthcoming during individual 
meetings with the treatment team. CVPR was involved with the 
patient prior to admission, and they continued to be a support 
to the patient while she was admitted. At the time of discharge, 
patient reported that symptoms of anxiety and depression had 
improved significantly, denied SI/HI, thoughts of self harm, and

testing this past ___ and had positive HCV Ab x2 during 
medicine admission. AST/ALT elevated in the ~1000 range, with

 1. Your right mastectomy incision may be left open to air, 
without a dressing.  Keep area clean.
 2. Clean around the drain site(s), where the tubing exits the 
skin, with soap and water.  
 3. Strip drain tubing, empty bulb(s), and record output(s) ___ 
times per day.  
 4. A written record of the daily output from each drain should 
be brought to every follow-up appointment. your drains will be 
removed as soon as possible when the daily output tapers off to 
an acceptable amount.  
 5. You may shower daily. No baths until instructed to do so by 
Dr. ___. 
 6. You may wear the surgibra that you were discharged with, for 
comfort.  You may also wear a light camisole. 
 .  
 Activity:  
 1. You may resume your regular diet.  
 2. DO NOT lift anything heavier than 5 pounds or engage in 
strenuous activity until instructed by Dr. ___.  
 .  
 Medications:  
 1. Resume your regular medications unless instructed otherwise 
and take any new meds as ordered.  
 2. You may take your prescribed pain medication for moderate to 
severe pain. You may switch to Tylenol or Extra Strength Tylenol 
for mild pain as directed on the packaging. Please note that 
Percocet and Vicodin have Tylenol as an active ingredient so do 
not take these meds with additional Tylenol.  
 3. Take prescription pain medications for pain not relieved by 
tylenol.  
 4. Take your antibiotic as prescribed.  
 5. Take Colace, 100 mg by mouth 2 times per day, while taking 
the prescription pain medication. You may use a different 
over-the-counter stool softener if you wish.  
 6.  Do not drive or operate heavy machinery while taking any 
narcotic pain medication. You may have constipation when taking 
narcotic pain medications (oxycodone, percocet, vicodin, 
hydrocodone, dilaudid, etc.); you should continue drinking 
fluids, you may take stool softeners, and should eat foods that 
are high in fiber.  
 7. Please avoid medications such as aspirin, advil, ibuprofen, 
motrin, Aleve, etc., for now.  You should only be taking your 
prescribed narcotic pain medication and/or tylenol.
.  
 Call the office IMMEDIATELY if you have any of the following:  
 1. Signs of infection: fever with chills, increased redness, 
swelling, warmth or tenderness at the surgical site, or unusual 
drainage from the incision(s).  
 2. A large amount of bleeding from the incision(s) or drain(s). 
 
 3. Fever greater than 101.5 oF  
 4. Severe pain NOT relieved by your medication.  
 .  
 Return to the ER if:  
 * If you are vomiting and cannot keep in fluids or your 
medications.  
 * If you have shaking chills, fever greater than 101.5 (F) 
degrees or 38 (C) degrees, increased redness, swelling or 
discharge from incision, chest pain, shortness of breath, or 
anything else that is troubling you.  
 * Any serious change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that 
concern you.  
 .  
 DRAIN DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS  
 You are being discharged with drains in place. Drain care is a 
clean procedure. Wash your hands thoroughly with soap and warm 
water before performing drain care. Perform drainage care twice 
a day. Try to empty the drain at the same time each day. Pull 
the stopper out of the drainage bottle and empty the drainage 
fluid into the measuring cup. Record the amount of drainage 
fluid on the record sheet. Reestablish drain suction.

Ms. ___ was admitted to L&D and was started on Pitocin for labor 
augmentation. She progressed to 7 cm at which time the Pitocin 
was turned off secondary to pattern abnormalities. The 
subsequent cervical exams were unchanged.  Pitocin was 
restarted, however, the fetal heart rate tracing was found to 
have recurrent variables. Subsequently an IUPC was placed to 
assess the adequacy of the contractions. The contractions were 
near adequate, however, Pitocin augmentation was not tolerated 
as there were frequent variables and occasionally late appearing 
decelerations with contractions. Given no cervical change in 6 
hours and fetal intolerance to Pitocin, the decision was made to 
proceed with primary low transverse cesarean delivery for arrest 
of dilation. Multiple large venous sinuses were present in the 
uterus leading to an elevated EBL of 1300mL. Please refer to the 
operative report for more details. Mother and infant were sent 
to the recovery room in stable condition.

tolerable. The ___ diabetes service followed her and 
recommended a GTT 6 weeks after delivery.

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. ___ was admitted to L+D for observation. Her blood pressures 
were initially elevated and she was given a single dose of PO 
labetalol 300 mg. A 24-hour urine collection was begun and 
returned with 1800 mg proteinuria, ruling her in for 
preeclampsia. Maternal Fetal Medicine was also consulted and 
recommended close inpatient monitoring of her labs and blood 
pressures. 

Her LFTs on admission were ALT 72, AST 42. A RUQ U/S was 
performed and was significant only for a small hemangioma in the 
liver. Viral titers were drawn, with negative CMV, EBV, and 
hepatitis. Her LFTs were repeated on ___, at which time they 
were 138 and 118 respectively. Given these findings as well as 
continued hypertension, the decision was made to proceed with 
delivery. She was kept NPO and transferred up to L+D that 
evening, where she underwent an uncomplicated LTCS. She 
tolerated this well and her twins were both taken to the NICU. 
She remained on L+D for magnesium and was then transferred to 
the postpartum floor. She received 24hrs of magnesium 
post-partum without issue. Her post-partum course was 
complicated by elevated blood pressures. She was treated 
initially with Labetalol. Nifedipine was added and her labetalol 
dose was increased on post-op day #4 with good effect. The 
patient's labs improved and clinically she was asymptomatic. She 
was discharged home in stable condition on post-op day #5 with a 
prescription for a blood pressure cuff in addition to her 
medications. She will follow up in 2 and 6wks.

This is the complicated case of a ___ year old single woman with 
unclear psychiatric diagnoses but with self reported history of 
depression, PTSD, ADHD, severe alcohol use disorder, stimulant 
use disorder, opiate use disorder on suboxone in the
past, s/p numerous psychiatric hospitalizations, who self

course notable for labile mood, anxiety, reported depressive

. 
Diagnostically, etiology of presentation is unclear to me at 
this time with differential diagnosis very broad-- includes 
substance induced mood disorder, complex trauma, underlying 
character pathology, primary mood disorder, and of course, her 
family history of ___ disease is concerning. Mental 
status examination was notable for intermittent psychomotor 
agitation, depressed mood that was very labile and reactive when 
she felt her needs were not being met, some distractability but 
with thought process that was linear, goal oriented on my 
examination. 
.
Overall, patient reported that on a prior regimen of gabapentin, 
bupropion, and dextroamphetamine doing quite well, with a 
reported period of ___ years of sustained abstinence while living 
in a sober living home. Of note, this was unconfirmed. While the 
patient was here, she was persistently insistent upon being 
prescribed stimulants during her admission, and was at times 
quite demanding and medication seeking. Given her recent relapse 
on alcohol and stimulants, it was determined that we did not 
have sufficient evidence to begin prescribing stimulants during 
the patient's admission and that the risks of restarting a 
stimulant outweighed the benefits.. For her mood and anxiety, 
she was started on Wellbutrin XL and Gabapentin, with eventual 
doses of Wellbutrin XL 300 mg daily and Gabapentin 800 mg PO 
TID. The patient overall tolerated well these medications and 
found them helpful for her mood and anxiety. In addition, for 
her alcohol cravings, she was started on Naltrexone. By time of 
discharge, the patient was prescribed a total of Naltrexone 50 
mg daily, and the patient found this medication to be helpful 
for her alcohol cravings. She tolerated this medication well 
without major side effects. In order to aid with the patient's 
nightmares, she was also started on Prazosin, which she 
tolerated well without major side effects and which she found 
helpful for nightmares. Finally, the patient was offered as 
needed Seroquel 50 mg TID for anxiety, agitation and insomnia, 
which the patient overall tolerated well and found helpful for 
the above stated symptoms. By time of discharge, patient's 
mental status was notable for improved mood, brighter affect, 
linear and goal-directed thought processes, denial of suicidal 
ideation, denial of violent ideations, denial of AVH, and fair 
insight and judgment. Of note, although the treatment team 
strongly recommended referral to residential substance abuse 
treatment vs. partial plus program, the patient declined these 
referrals, stating she preferred to follow up with outpatient 
providers. Given her denial of SI, strong desire to leave the 
hospital and capacity to make this decision, thought process 
that was linear, logical, goal and future oriented (patient 
wanting to continue outpatient treatment so she could regain 
custody of her son), she was discharged prior to expiration of 
her 3 day notice with plan to stay with her mother over the 
weekend and then live with her friend. 
.
3. SUBSTANCE USE DISORDERS:
#) Nicotine Use Disorder 
During her admission, the patient was provided both with smoking 
cessation counseling and nicotine supplementation via Nicotine 
Patch 14 mg TD daily. At discharge, patient was prescribed 
nicotine patch for further smoking cessation aid and provided 
with follow up for ongoing smoking cessation counseling. 
.
#) Alcohol Use Disorder
Patient on admission was monitored for signs and symptoms of 
alcohol withdrawal, which she did not demonstrate. The patient 
was prescribed Naltrexone during her admission, with a final 
dose of 50 mg upon discharge. The patient tolerated well this 
medication, and she found it helpful for alcohol cravings. We 
encouraged use of AA upon DC for further sobriety support. 
.
#) Stimulant and opioid use disorder
Patient was provided with education regarding the deleterious 
effects of substance use upon mental health, and was encouraged 
to utilize AA/NA upon DC for further sobriety support. Given 
concerns for recent relapse on methamphetamine and lack of 
collateral information to confirm diagnosis, the risk of 
initiation stimulant prescription during admission was 
determined to outweigh the benefit of starting a stimulant at 
this time. We would recommend that outpatient providers proceed 
cautiously prior to initiating stimulants. Of note, patient 
stated she had Narcan and so she was not prescribed this at 
discharge. 
.

FINDINGS:  There is a live intrauterine pregnancy with a 
crown-rump length of 5.4 cm corresponding to a gestational age

bilaterally.  There is no free fluid.  The placenta is 
posterior.  There is note made of a small subchorionic hematoma 
which measures 2 cm x 3 cm.
IMPRESSION:
1.  Live IUP with size equal to dates.  Age based on ultrasound 
is 12 weeks 1 day, which corresponds satisfactorily to that 
based on LMP of 11 weeks 4 days.
2.  2 cm x 3 cm subchorionic hematoma near the uterine fundus.

___ CTA PELVIS
FINDINGS:  The lower uterine segment, cervix, and upper vagina 
are expanded by a heterogeneous dense collection of likely 
blood/blood products.  Unusually this collection does not extend 
into the upper/fundal portions of the endometrial cavity which 
appear relatively normal both in size and CT appearance. Given 
this appearance and thinning of the surrounding myometrium, 
concern is raised for possible eccentric location of these blood 
products, perhaps within the uterine wall.  In total it measures 
approximately 7.9 x 4.9 x 5.9 cm.  As mentioned, the fundal 
portion of the uterus appears unremarkable.  
The bladder contains Foley catheter and is otherwise 
unremarkable.  A trace amount of simple fluid is seen in the 
adnexa.  No dependent free pelvic fluid is seen.  The imaged 
bowel is unremarkable.  No free intraperitoneal air is seen.  No 
pelvic or inguinal adenopathy is seen.
CT arteriogram and venogram were also performed and demonstrate 
normal vessels without evidence of active extravasation or 
pooling of contrast to suggest active large vessel bleeding.
OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  There is no lytic or sclerotic bony lesion 
concerning for osseous malignant process.
IMPRESSION:  Expansion of the lower uterine segment and cervix 
by large heterogeneously dense collection of likely blood or 
blood products with relative sparing of the upper uterus, 
raising concern for possible eccentric location of this 
collection including intramural location rather than endometrial 
location.  

___ UTERINE ARTERY EMBOLIZATION (PRELIMINARY REPORT)
FINDINGS:
1. Initial pelvic arteriography demonstrated the presence of a 
contrast blush in the expected location of the lower uterine 
segment on the left.
2. Selective left uterine arteriography demonstrated expected 
hypervascularity of a gravid uterus and confirmed the presence 
of a blush in the lower uterine segment on the left.
3. Post-embolization arteriography demonstrated near complete 
occlusion of the left uterine artery with no residual blush 
seen. Some normal supply to the upper portion of the uterus on 
the left persisted.
4. Right uterine artery arteriography demonstrated expected 
hypervascularity of a gravid uterus and also demonstrated 
collateral supply to the region of blush in the lower uterine 
segment on the left.
5. Post-embolization arteriography demonstrated occlusion of the 
right uterine artery with no residual supply to the left sided 
blush.
IMPRESSION:
1. Bilateral uterine artery embolization with gelatin sponge for 
refractory uterine bleeding following D&E.
2. A small area of blush from the left uterine artery was seen 
in the expected location of the lower uterine segment on the 
left with collateral supply from the right uterine artery. The 
blush was no longer present following embolization.

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms ___ was admitted to the gynecology service for management 
of her hemorrhage and threatened abortion. Her bleeding had 
stabilized on admission, she was admitted for observation. 

After extensive counseling and consideration of her various 
options, she elected to terminate the pregnancy. On hospital day 
#2 a dilation and curettage was performed; please see operative 
report for full details of the procedure. There was an 
intraoperative hemorrhage with an estimated blood loss of 1000mL 
necessitating uterotonics, intrauterine Foley balloon and 
vaginal packing for tamponade. She was transfused two units of 
pRBCs and transferred to the PACU in stable condition.

In the PACU, she gradually became hypotensive to the ___. 
There was a small amount of bleeding from the intrauterine Foley 
catheter; however, a bedside ultrasound revealed a significant 
hematometra. She was resuscitated with IV fluids, two additional 
units of pRBCs and Hespan. She did not have any laboratory 
evidence of DIC and she returned to the operating room for 
repeat dilation and curettage under ultrasound guidance. Please 
see operative report for full details of the procedure. The 
hematometra was evacuated and an intrauterine Bakri balloon was 
placed. She was trasnfused one additional unit of pRBCs, two 
units FFP, and two units cryoprecipitate, and she was 
transferred to the PACU in stable condition.

She remained clinically stable overnight. On hospital day #3 
(postoperative day #1) the Bakri balloon was noted to be in the 
vagina and it was removed. Her hematocrit declined over the 
course of the day, from 31% to 23%. She was transfused one 
additional unit of pRBCs. A bedside ultrasound revealed clot in 
the uterus. Given the concern for continued bleeding, after 
extensive counseling she underwent bilateral uterine artery 
embolization.

Following the embolization procedure she remained clinically 
stable for the rest of her hospitalization. She passed the clot 
and her hematocrit remained at 27% over hospital days #4 and 5 
(postoperative days #2 and 3). She was given Depo Provera for 
contraception and was discharged home on hospital day #5 in 
stable condition, with close outpatient followup scheduled.

Dear Ms ___,

You were transferred to ___ 
after you went to the emergency room with bleeding in pregnancy.

the uterus and stop the bleeding. You received transfusions and 
the uterine arteries were embolized. Your bleeding stopped and 
your blood counts are stable. It is now safe for you to be 
discharged home. We have given you a prescription for iron to 
take at home.

It would be extremely dangerous for you to become pregnant 
again. You were given an injection of Depo-Provera 
(contraceptive) prior to discharge and this injection will 
protect you from pregnancy for 3 months. Please discuss 
contraception with Dr. ___ you return for your 
appointment. You were given a taxi voucher for transportation to 
this appointment.

* Take your medications as prescribed.  
* Do not take more than 4000mg acetaminophen (APAP) in 24 hrs  
* No strenuous activity until your post-op appointment  
* Nothing in the vagina (no tampons, no douching, no sex), no 
heavy lifting of objects >10 lbs for 6 weeks.  
* You may eat a regular diet

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. ___ was admitted to the GYN service for inpatient 
management of pelvic inflammatory disease. She was febrile to 
105 in the ED.  Her WBC was 16 at admission and without bands. 
She was started on IV gentamicin and clindamycin upon admission. 
Ultrasound was concerning for focal area of infection within the 
myometrium.  However, this was not amenable to drainage and the 
patient was improving clinically on antibiotics. Thus, the 
decision was made to continue antibiotics only.  Her blood 
cultures and STI panel were negative. 

She was treated with IV gentamicin and clindamycin until she 
remained afebrile for 48 hours.  She was then started on oral 
clindamycin for a two week course. While in the hospital her 
pain improved.  She was discharged home on hospital day 4 in 
good condition with follow-up.

team agreed that her repeated episodes of LOC was more likely

patient along with surgery decided to have an elective 
cholecystectomy after a period of weeks of recovery at home.  
She will contact the surgery office to schedule a two week f/u 
visit and then plan on surgery in approx 6 weeks. 

During this admission she also received IV antibiotics including 
zosyn, which was discontinued at discharge.  She received cipro 
in the ED and dumped her breast milk 48hr after this and also 
dumped her breast milk for 24h after receiving anesthesia for 
her ERCP.

She tolerated a regular meal and had no abdominal pain on the 
day of discharge. Her LFTs also substantiall improved following 
the ERCP.

severe and unremitting. Her platelet count fell from 117 on 
admission to 76 on hospital day #4, and she developed 
hypertension, with two blood pressures >6 hours apart that were 
>140/90. On ___ she was therefore diagnosed with atypical severe 
pre-eclampsia, started on magnesium sulfate for seizure 
prophylaxis and labor induction was begun.

Postpartum she continued to have a severe headache poorly 
responsive to pain medication. On postpartum day 2 an MRI was 
obtained and neurology was consulted. MRI was significant for 
pachymeningeal enhancement compatible with intracranial 
hypotension and she was diagnosed with a dural tear likely 
secondary to the upper respiratory tract infection she had 
immediately preceeding onset of these HA. The headaches were 
then treated with caffeine and IV fluids and responded well. She 
was discharged on PPD#5 once her headaches had reduced 
significantly in intensity and had responded well to po 
medications.

She did not require any anti-hypertensives during her postpartum 
course.

Of note, pt was found to have a leukopenia beginning on PPD#2, 
felt likely secondary to her pre-existing immune deficiency 
which stabalized at a WBC of 3 (nadir 2.6).

1000cc of urine.  Her foley was replaced and left in overnight.

voided.  The rest of her post-operative course was uneventful

discharged on POD#1 in good condition.

Dear Ms. ___
.
You were admitted to the gynecologic oncology service after 
undergoing the procedures listed below. You have recovered well 
after your operation, and the team feels that you are safe to be 
discharged home. Please follow these instructions: 
. 
Abdominal instructions:  
* Take your medications as prescribed.  
* Do not drive while taking narcotics.  
* Do not combine narcotic and sedative medications or alcohol.  
* Do not take more than 4000mg acetaminophen (APAP) in 24 hrs.  
* No strenuous activity until your post-op appointment.  
* Nothing in the vagina (no tampons, no douching, no sex) for 12 
weeks.  
* No heavy lifting of objects >10 lbs for 6 weeks.  
* You may eat a regular diet.
* It is safe to walk up stairs.

cystogram that was negative for a bladder leak. She was also 
treated for a UTI with keflex which was transitioned to 
augmentin for a total 7 day course (enterococcus).

mood disorder and multiple medications including lithium and 
effexor that were abruptly discontinued a few weeks prior to 
delivery. The patient declined psychiatry eval and did not want 
to resume any of her prior medications. She will follow up with

strenuous activity until instructed by Dr. ___.  
 .  
 Medications:  
 1. Resume your regular medications unless instructed otherwise 
and take any new meds as ordered.  
 2. You may take your prescribed pain medication for moderate to 
severe pain. You may switch to Tylenol or Extra Strength Tylenol 
for mild pain as directed on the packaging. Please note that 
Percocet and Vicodin have Tylenol as an active ingredient so do 
not take these meds with additional Tylenol.  
 4. Take prescription pain medications for pain not relieved by

the prescription pain medication. You may use a different 
over-the-counter stool softener if you wish.  
 7.  Do not drive or operate heavy machinery while taking any 
narcotic pain medication. You may have constipation when taking 
narcotic pain medications (oxycodone, percocet, vicodin, 
hydrocodone, dilaudid, etc.); you should continue drinking 
fluids, you may take stool softeners, and should eat foods that 
are high in fiber.  
.  
 Call the office IMMEDIATELY if you have any of the following:  
 1. Signs of infection: fever with chills, increased redness, 
swelling, warmth or tenderness at the surgical site, or unusual 
drainage from the incision(s).

medications.

degrees or 38 (C) degrees, increased redness, swelling or 
discharge from incision, chest pain, shortness of breath, or 
anything else that is troubling you.

concern you.

 Pain  
 It is expected that you will have pain after surgery and this 
pain will gradually improved over the first week or so you are 
home. You will especially have pain when changing positions and 
with movement. You should continue to take 2 Extra Strength 
Tylenol (___) for pain every 8 hours around the clock and you 
may also take Advil (Ibuprofen) 600mg every hours for 7 days. 
Please do not take more than 3000mg of Tylenol in 24 hours or 
any other medications that contain Tylenol such as cold 
medication. Do not drink alcohol while or Tylenol. Please take 
Advil with food. If these medications are not controlling your 
pain to a point where you can ambulate and preform minor tasks, 
you should take a dose of the narcotic pain medication 
oxycodone. Please take this only if needed for pain. Do not take 
with any other sedating medications or alcohol. Do not drive a 
car if taking narcotic pain medications.  
 Activity  
 You may feel weak or "washed out" for up to 6 weeks after 
surgery. No heavy lifting greater than a gallon of milk for 3 
weeks. You may climb stairs. You may go outside and walk, but 
avoid traveling long distances until you speak with your 
surgical team at your first follow-up visit. Your surgical team 
will clear you for heavier exercise and activity as the observe 
your progress at your follow-up appointment. You should only 
drive a car on your own if you are off narcotic pain medications 
and feel as if your reaction time is back to normal so you can 
react appropriately while driving.  

Thank you for allowing us to participate in your care! Our hope 
is that you will have a quick return to your life and usual 
activities.

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms ___ was admitted to the antepartum service with elevated 
blood pressures.  She was started on nifedipine and a 24-hour 
urine collection was begun. Upon transfer to the antepartum 
floor she complained of a headache and had elevated blood 
pressures, so she was brought back to Labor and Delivery for 
closer monitoring. Labs revealed low platelets and transaminitis 
as well as new hematuria. One dose of betamethasone was given 
and neonatology was consulted. Her blood pressures continued to 
be elevated and she was begun on a magnesium drip. Repeat labs 
revealed worsening transaminases and decreasing platelets. Her 
epigastric pain continued, and the decision was made to proceed 
with delivery given her worsening clinical status. On hospital 
day #2 Ms ___ underwent classical cesarean delivery; see 
operative report for complete details of the case. 

Ms ___ was kept on a magnesium drip for 24 hours postpartum. 
Given her initial presenting symptoms of vomiting and diarrhea, 
as well as her hemolysis and thrombocytopenia, hematology was 
consulted and found her clinical picture to be most consistent 
with HELLP syndrome. A stool culture was negative.

For her thrombocytopenia Ms ___ received 1 unit of platelets 
intraoperatively and a second unit of platelets on labor and 
delivery, as well as 2 doses of dexamethasone. Her platelet 
count nadired at 27,000 on postpartum day #0 and subsequently 
stabilized and improved over her hospitalization. She was never 
coagulopathic. 

Ms ___ also had hemolysis as well as blood loss anemia and 
on postpartum day #1 received 2 units of packed red blood cells 
with an appropriate rise in her hematocrit from 18.4% to 24.2%. 
Her LDH improved beginning on the evening of postpartum day #0, 
as did her trasaminitis and hyperbilirubinemia. 

On postpartum day #1 Ms ___ was transferred to the 
postpartum floor, where her labs and symptoms continued to 
improve. Her blood pressures were within goal on 200mg labetalol 
TID. Her pain was managed initially with a Dilaudid PCA and then 
with oxycodone, Tylenol, and gabapentin, and she was advised to 
avoid NSAIDs. Her postoperative course was subsequently

* Take your medications as prescribed.  
* Do not drive while taking narcotics.  
* Take a stool softener such as colace while taking narcotics to 
prevent constipation.  
* Do not combine narcotic and sedative medications or alcohol.  
* Do not take more than 4000mg acetaminophen (APAP) in 24 hrs.  
* No strenuous activity until your post-op appointment.  
* Nothing in the vagina for 6 weeks.
* No heavy lifting of objects >10 lbs for 6 weeks.  
* You may eat a regular diet.  
* You may walk up and down stairs.  

Incision care:  
* You may shower and allow soapy water to run over incision; no 
scrubbing of incision. No bath tubs for 6 weeks.  
* If you have steri-strips, leave them on. They will fall off on 
their own or be removed during your followup visit.   

Call your doctor for:  
* fever > 100.4  
* severe abdominal pain  
* difficulty urinating  
* vaginal bleeding requiring >1 pad/hr  
* abnormal vaginal discharge  
* redness or drainage from incision  
* nausea/vomiting where you are unable to keep down fluids/food 
or your medication

symptomatic relief of her preterm contractions, however, her 
contractions persisted and she developed a severe headache. Her 
preterm contractions ultimately resolved on HD#2 without any 
further tocolytic therapy. 

During evaluation of her preterm contractions, she was noted to 
have bacterial vaginosis and was started on Flagyl for a 7 day 
course. 

Given her persistently elevated blood pressures and the severe 
headache in the morning of HD#2, her Labetalol was increased to 
300mg PO TID and she underwent a pre-eclmapsia evaluation. Her 
serum labs were noted to be normal, however, her 24 hour urien 
was positive at 770mg (baseline 198mg) and she was diagnosed 
with severe pre-eclampsia by blood pressures. 

She was made betamethasone complete and had intensive inpatient 
management in house for her cHTN with superimposed severe 
pre-eclampsia. Her Labetalol was ultimately titrated up to 400 
TID.

Given that she was diagnosed with bacterial vaginosis on 
admission, it was felt that her significant proteinuria may have 
been inaccurate and rather a reflection of vaginal leukorrhea.  
Therefore, a 24 hour urine was repeated on ___ after 
treatment with metronidazole.  This returned with 215mg of 
protein. Given the stabilization of her blood pressures, absence 
of PEC sx, stability of normal HELLP labs, and minimal 
proteinuria, she was offered to stay in house versus be 
discharged to home with twice weekly testing.  She chooses the 
latter and is discharged in good condition with scheduled 
follow-up.

___ yo pregnant (week 11) F with PSA who was referred by out 
patient provider for increased depression, SI and detox in 
context of recent crack cocaine and EtOH use, homelessness and

Psychiatric: While the patient initially reported suicidal 
ideation and depression, she denied suicidality throughout her 
hospitalization. She reported that she would be safe to leave an 
inpatient setting, but wanted inpatient treatment of her 
substance abuse; she initially did not express any confidence 
that she could remain abstinent from drugs during the rest of 
her pregnancy if not in an inpatient setting. The patient 
continued taking Prozac 40mg daily during her stay. Haldol 
(1.5mg) was added to her medication regimen, but she reported 
that it made her tongue feel stiff, which was sucsessfully 
treated with Benadryl, and caused her to become tearful. 
Therefore, she refused to take Haldol. After stopping the 
Haldol, she admitted to having more thoughts of using drugs. She 
was unable to reconcile that Haldol may be good for her baby if 
it prevents her from abusing drugs. She did not appear to 
understand the risk of using cocaine and EtOH would be 
significantly more harmful to her baby compared to side effects 
of Haldol. She reluctantly agreed to use lamotrigine and denied

Discharge Instructions:
 
Depression: Tips to Help Yourself

As your healthcare providers help treat your depression, you can 
also help yourself. Keep in mind that your illness affects you 
emotionally, physically, mentally, and socially. So full 
recovery will take time. Take care of your body and your soul, 
and be patient with yourself as you get better.

Be With Others
___ isolate yourselfyoull only feel worse. Try to be with 
other people. And take part in fun activities when you can. Go 
to a movie, ballgame, religious service, or social event. Talk 
openly with people you can trust. And accept help when its 
offered.

Keep Your Perspective
Depression can cloud your judgment. So wait until you feel 
better before making major life decisions, such as changing 
jobs, moving, or getting married or divorced.

This illness is not your fault. ___ blame yourself for your 
depression.

Recovering from depression is a process. ___ be discouraged if 
it takes some time to feel better.

Depression saps your energy and concentration. So you wont be 
able to do all the things you used to do. Set small goals and do 
what you can.

Take Care of Your Body
People with depression often lose the desire to take care of 
themselves. That only makes their problems worse. During 
treatment and afterward, make a point to:

Exercise. Its a great way to take care of your body. And 
studies have shown that exercise helps fight depression.

Avoid drugs and alcohol. These may ease the ___ in the short 
term. But theyll only make your problems worse in the long run.

Get relief from stress. Ask your healthcare provider for 
relaxation exercises and techniques to help relieve stress.

Eat right. A balanced and healthy diet helps keep your body 
healthy.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms ___,

You have undergone a laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy, recovered 
in the hospital and are now prepared for discharge to home with 
the following instructions:

Please call your surgeon or return to the Emergency Department 
if you develop a fever greater than ___ F, shaking chills, chest 
pain, difficulty breathing, pain with breathing, cough, a rapid 
heartbeat, dizziness, severe abdominal pain, pain unrelieved by 
your pain medication, a change in the nature or severity of your 
pain, severe nausea, vomiting, abdominal bloating, severe 
diarrhea, inability to eat or drink, foul smelling or colorful 
drainage from your incisions, redness, swelling from your 
incisions, or any other symptoms which are concerning to you.  

Diet: Stay on Stage II diet until your follow up appointment; 
this stage was previously called stage III and consists of 
protein shakes, sugar free smooth pudding/ yogurt, etc; please 
refer to your work book for detailed instructions. Do not self- 
advance your diet and avoid drinking with a straw or chewing 
gum. To avoid dehydration, remember to sip small amounts of 
fluid frequently throughout the day to reach a goal of 
approximately ___ mL per day. Please note the following signs 
of dehydration: dry mouth, rapid heartbeat, feeling dizzy or 
faint, dark colored urine, infrequent urination.  

Medication Instructions:  
Please refer to the medication list provided with your discharge 
paperwork for detailed instruction regarding your home and newly 
prescribed medications.  
Some of the new medications you will be taking include:  
1. Pain medication: You will receive a prescription for liquid 
acetaminophen (Tylenol), do not exceed 3000 mg per 24 hour 
period.  
2. Antacids: You will be taking famotidine tablets, 20 mg twice 
daily, for one month. This medicine reduces stomach acid 
production. Please crush.  
3. You must not use NSAIDS (non-steroidal anti-inflammatory 
drugs) unless approved by your Weight Loss Surgery team. 
Examples include, but are not limited to Aleve, Arthrotec, 
aspirin, Bufferin, diclofenac, Ecotrin, etodolac, ibuprofen, 
Indocin, indomethacin, Feldene, ketorolac, meclofenamate, 
meloxicam, Midol, Motrin, nambumetone, Naprosyn, Naproxen, 
Nuprin, oxaprozin, Piroxicam, Relafen, Toradol and Voltaren. 
These agents may cause bleeding and ulcers in your digestive 
system. If you are unclear whether a medication is considered an 
NSAID, please ask call your nurse or ask your pharmacist.  
4. Vitamins/ minerals: You may resume a chewable multivitamin, 
however, please discuss when to resume additional vitamin and 
mineral supplements with your bariatric dietitian. 
 
Activity:  
You should continue walking frequently throughout the day right 
after surgery; you may climb stairs.  
You may resume moderate exercise at your discretion, but avoid 
performing abdominal exercises or lifting items greater than10 
to 15 pounds for six weeks.  

Wound Care:  
You may remove any remaining gauze from over your incisions. You 
will have thin paper strips (Steri-Strips) over your incision; 
please, remove any remaining Steri-Strips seven to 10 days after 
surgery.  
You may shower 48 hours following your surgery; avoid scrubbing 
your incisions and gently pat them dry. Avoid tub baths or 
swimming until cleared by your surgeon. You may wear your 
abdominal binder as needed for comfort.  
If there is clear drainage from your incisions, cover with 
clean, dry gauze.  
Please call the doctor if you have increased pain, swelling, 
redness, cloudy, bloody or foul smelling drainage from the 
incision sites.  
Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area for up to 24 
months.  
Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told 
otherwise.  

Please follow up with PCP for vision changes.

Surgery

·     You underwent surgery to remove a brain lesion from your

to cover it when outside.

redness, fever, or drainage. 

 
Activity

·     We recommend that you avoid heavy lifting, running, 
climbing, or other strenuous exercise until your follow-up 
appointment.

·     You make take leisurely walks and slowly increase your 
activity at your own pace once you are symptom free at rest. 
___ try to do too much all at once.

·     No driving while taking any narcotic or sedating 
medication. 

·     If you experienced a seizure while admitted, you are NOT 
allowed to drive by law. 

·     No contact sports until cleared by your neurosurgeon. You 
should avoid contact sports for 6 months. 

 

Medications

·     Do NOT take any blood thinning medication (Aspirin, 
Ibuprofen, Plavix, Coumadin) until cleared by the neurosurgeon. 

·     You may use Acetaminophen (Tylenol) for minor discomfort 
if you are not otherwise restricted from taking this medication.

 
What You ___ Experience:

·     You may experience headaches and incisional pain. 

·     You may also experience some post-operative swelling 
around your face and eyes. This is normal after surgery and most 
noticeable on the second and third day of surgery.  You apply 
ice or a cool or warm washcloth to your eyes to help with the 
swelling. The swelling will be its worse in the morning after 
laying flat from sleeping but decrease when up. 

·     You may experience soreness with chewing. This is normal 
from the surgery and will improve with time. Softer foods may be 
easier during this time. 

·     Feeling more tired or restlessness is also common.

·     Constipation is common. Be sure to drink plenty of fluids 
and eat a high-fiber diet. If you are taking narcotics 
(prescription pain medications), try an over-the-counter stool 
softener.

Brief Hospital Course:
On ___, Ms. ___ was admitted to the antepartum service at 
32w0d gestation with hematuria and urinary retention. Her UA was 
concerning for a UTI and she was started on IV ceftriaxone. 

Medicine was consulted who recommended LFTs/bili/LDH/haptoglobin 
which returned normal to screen for hemolysis given recent 
exposure to macrobid in the setting of known G6PD deficiency. 
She underwent a renal ultrasound which did show 2 nonobstructive 
calculi bilaterally with no evidence of hydronephrosis and 
normal ureteral jets bilaterally.

An obstetric ultrasound was performed which did not show 
evidence of a placenta accrete. 

Urology was consulted. A bladder ultrasound was performed which 
was notable for a 6x2x4cm clot within the bladder. A CT urogram 
was performed which showed no abnormalities within the kidneys, 
ureters, or bladder; there was a small circular hypodensity at 
the left UVJ felt to be a phlebolith or calcified vessel. A 
cystoscopy was performed which showed a normal bladder with 
small clots, a normal left retrograde pyelogram, and no filling 
defects or obvious causes for hematuria.

___ was also consulted. Given hemodynamic stability and 
reassuring fetal testing, delivery was not recommended. 
Betamethasone for fetal lung maturity was recommended in case a 
preterm delivery is indicated however pt declined. 

Nephrology was consulted with low suspicion for glomerular 
hematuria, and recommended an MRI to evaluate for AV 
malformation, small renal/ureteral mass, or unilateral renal 
vein thrombosis. This was deferred given resolution of her 
hematuria on ___. 

A multi-disciplinary meeting was held on ___ with ___, 
medicine, radiology, and urology. Alternative diagnoses such as 
evaluation for an AVM by using angiography was discussed. 
However, given resolution of hematuria, additional imaging or 
intervention was deferred. 

The patient's HCT and Cr remained stable throughout her hospital 
course. She had reassuring daily fetal testing. On ___, she 
continued to have resolution of her hematuria and she was 
discharged with close outpatient follow up.

Patient was transferred to the MICU on ___ due to worsening

fever to 101.3 several hours after transfusion.  Blood cultures 
were sent, which were negative.  A transfusion work-up was sent, 
and recommendations were made that she could receive platelets

___ showed a EF of 25%.  Cardiology was consulted, who 
recommended keeping her fluid balance even.  She was placed on 
fluid restrictions, gently diuresed with lasix for one day.  Her 
systolic function improved on repeat ECHO on ___.  The patient 
initially required ___ NC to maintain her sats in the mid ___.

to transfer to the antepartum floor on ___...
